titleStmt.title: The effect of midgap states on the magnetic exchange interaction mediated by a рЭСС-wave superconductor

affiliation.orgName: Norwegian University of Science and Technology
Norwegian University of Science and Technology
Norwegian University of Science and Technology

analytic.title: The effect of midgap states on the magnetic exchange interaction mediated by a рЭСС-wave superconductor

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We theoretically study the indirect interaction between two ferromagnetic contacts located on the surface of a рЭСС-wave superconductor. When the magnets are connected to a {010} edge of the superconductor we find an oscillating RKKY interaction that varies in sign as the distance between the magnetic contacts is varied. However, when coupling the magnets to an {110} edge of the superconductor, we find that the presence of midgap states qualitatively changes the results. The ground state of the system is then found to always favor alignment of the magnets as this configuration most strongly suppresses the midgap states, leading to a larger condensation energy which dominates over the intrinsic RKKY interaction.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Superconductors with рЭСС-wave symmetry have an anisotropic order parameter which drops to zero along some nodal directions [1][2][3]. A {110} edge of a рЭСС рЭС• 2 -рЭС¶ 2 superconductor has been shown to feature dispersionless surface states with zero energy, called midgap states [4]. The appearance of midgap states for such an edge is related to the fact that the order parameter in a 45 вАҐ rotated coordinate system takes the form рЭСС рЭС• рЭС¶ , introducing opposite signs for the pair potential experienced by particles undergoing specular and Andreev reflections at the surface. The {110} edge also gives rise to a zero bias conductance peak [5], which is a result of the presence of the midgap states [6]. Such a zero bias conductance peak has been experimentally observed in the high-рЭСЗ рЭСР cuprate superconductors [7][8][9][10] and has been important in determining the pairing symmetry of these superconductors.

The indirect exchange interaction between two localized spins, mediated by the itinerant electrons of a host material, was first introduced by Ruderman, Kittel, Kasuya and Yosida, and is known as the RKKY interaction [11][12][13]. In this indirect exchange interaction, itinerant electrons of the host material scatter off a localized spin, and the wave functions of the scattered electrons interfere with each other giving rise to alternating regions with high density of spin up/down. This leads to the well-known RKKY oscillations in the spin-spin interaction strength which decrease with the distance рЭСЕ between the two localized spins as рЭСЕ -рЭРЈ , where рЭРЈ is the dimensionality of the system. RKKY interaction has been investigated in various materials ranging from normal metals [11,13], to oneand two-dimensional electron gases [14,15], two-dimensional structures like graphene [16][17][18][19][20], and topological insulators [21][22][23].

For a system consisting of magnetic impurities embedded in a superconductor, the influence of superconductivity on the indirect impurity-impurity interaction has also been studied in the literature [24,25]. For a conventional рЭС†-wave superconductor, when the distance between the impurities is larger than the superconducting coherence length, the interaction between them is found to be antiferromagnetic in character and suppressed compared to the normal metal case. The suppression is caused by the superconducting gap reducing the number of states close to the Fermi level that can mediate the interaction. Below the coherence length, the behavior is similar to the normal metal case with an oscillatory RKKY interaction that changes sign with distance. However, non-perturbative treatments have shown that Yu-Shiba-Rusinov (YSR) bound states can give rise to mainly antiferromagnetic behavior even at distances shorter than the coherence length [26]. Further, for impurities on the surface of a three-dimensional topological insulator with proximity-induced s-wave superconductivity, the RKKY interaction favors the impurity spins to be in-plane and antiparallel [27]. For a spin-valve structure consisting of two ferromagnetic insulators connected by an рЭС†-wave superconductor, experiments have shown that anti-alignment of the magnets is still favored [28].

Conventional рЭС†-wave superconductors do however typically have coherence lengths far exceeding the decay length of the RKKY interaction. On the other hand, рЭСС-wave superconductors can feature very short coherence lengths of the order of nanometers [29], offering an intriguing platform for studying the interplay between superconductivity and RKKY interaction, as the characteristic length scales of both phenomena are comparable. RKKY interaction between magnetic impurities mediated by a d-wave superconductor with an anisotropic order parameter of the type рЭСС рЭС• 2 -рЭС¶ 2 has lead to similar behavior as in the s-wave case [30]. Further, for a spin-valve structure involving a рЭСС рЭС• 2 -рЭС¶ 2 superconductor, nodal quasiparticles close to the Fermi surface have been observed to mediate interaction that favors anti-alignment of the magnetic insulators for a sufficiently large superconductor thickness [31].

As the gapped band structure of a superconductor suppresses the RKKY interaction, it is of interest to investigate the effect the presence of midgap states have on the interaction. We therefore consider a рЭСС рЭС• 2 -рЭС¶ 2 superconductor and calculate the exchange interaction between two ferromagnetic contacts located on a diagonal {110} edge, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The superconductor is modelled by an extended BCS tight binding Hamiltonian on a square lattice and connected to the metallic magnets through a hopping term across the interface. The results are obtained through a self-consistent solution of the Bogoliubov-de Gennes (BdG) equations [32]. To put the results into context, we consider the cases of a normal metal and an isotropic рЭС†-wave superconductor, in addition to the рЭСС-wave superconductor. In all three cases, we investigate the interaction between ferromagnetic contacts located on both diagonal and FIG. 1: Schematic illustration of a рЭСС-wave superconductor with midgap surface states mediating the indirect exchange interaction between two ferromagnetic contacts. We will consider configurations where the magnetization of the two magnets is either parallel (P) or antiparallel (AP). For comparison, the ferromagnetic contacts will also be attached to the lower, nondiagonal, edge of the superconductor. The lengths indicated on the figure will in the following be specified by the number of atomic distances. non-diagonal edges.

For magnetic contacts located on a diagonal edge of a рЭССwave superconductor, we find that the system always favors alignment of the two magnets. The variation in the strength of the magnetic exchange interaction as we vary the distance between the magnets is small compared to the magnitude of the interaction itself. We attribute these results to the aligned magnets more efficiently suppressing the midgap states than the anti-aligned configuration. Although the aligned magnets induce a stronger spin-splitting in the superconductor, suppressing the gap, the reduction of the midgap states leads to an overall larger gap and increased condensation energy. The parallel magnet configuration is therefore the ground state of the system.

The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. II we introduce the model and methodology. Then, in Sec. III we present and discuss the results. Finally, in Sec. IV we provide a summary of the findings. The phase diagram of our рЭСС-wave superconductor model for a square system with continuous boundary conditions is included in the Appendix.

div.head: II. MODEL AND METHODS
div.p: By means of a tight binding Hamiltonian on a square lattice, we model the attractive electron-electron interaction in a superconductor
div.formula: рЭРї рЭСЖрЭРґ = - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ,
div.p: Here, рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ is a creation operator creating an electron with spin рЭЫЉ on lattice site рЭСЦ = (рЭСЦ рЭС• , рЭСЦ рЭС¶ ). The hopping amplitude is denoted by рЭС° рЭСЦ рЭСЧ , and рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ is the chemical potential. The third term represents on-site attractive interactions between opposite spins, where the number operator is рЭСЫ рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ = рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ . This term gives rise to conventional spin singlet isotropic рЭС†wave superconductivity. The fourth and fifth terms represent nearest neighbour interaction between opposite or equal spins, respectively. These terms can give rise to рЭСС-wave, рЭСЭ-wave or extended s-wave pairing for an attractive interaction potential. For the purposes of this article, we will set рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ to zero as we will not be interested in the possibility of equal spin pairing. As shown in the Appendix, the above model without рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ can give rise to a рЭСС-wave superconductor for a suitable choice of chemical potential.

div.head: A. Analytical Methods
div.p: Through a mean-field treatment, we simplify the interaction terms. The on-site part of the interaction becomes
div.formula: - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСИ рЭСЦ рЭСЫ рЭСЦвЖС рЭСЫ рЭСЦвЖУ = - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСИ рЭСЦ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС + рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ -рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ .(2)
div.p: Defining the superconducting gap for the on-site interaction as ќФ рЭСЦ = -рЭСИ рЭСЦ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС , we obtain
div.formula: - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСИ рЭСЦ рЭСЫ рЭСЦвЖС рЭСЫ рЭСЦвЖУ = вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ ќФ рЭСЦ + рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС ќФ * рЭСЦ ) + рЭРї рЭСЖ 0 ,(3)
div.p: where we have defined
div.formula: рЭРї рЭСЖ 0 = вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ |ќФ рЭСЦ | 2 рЭСИ рЭСЦ .(4)
div.p: The on-site interaction рЭСИ рЭСЦ will be taken to a constant рЭСИ вЙ• 0 in the superconductor, and zero elsewhere. Once again, performing a mean-field treatment, the attractive nearest neighbor interaction term becomes
div.formula: вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉвЙ†рЭЫЉ рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭСЫ рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСЫ рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ = вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉвЙ†рЭЫЉ рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ + рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ -рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ ).(5)
div.p: We then define the nearest neighbor pairing amplitude
div.formula: рЭРє рЭЫЉрЭЫЉ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ = рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ ,(6)
div.p: transforming Eq. ( 5) into
div.formula: вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉвЙ†рЭЫЉ рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭРє рЭЫЉрЭЫЉ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ + рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ (рЭРє рЭЫЉрЭЫЉ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ ) вА† ) + рЭРї рЭСС 0 ,(7)
div.p: where
div.formula: рЭРї рЭСС 0 = - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ, рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉвЙ†рЭЫЉ рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ |рЭРє рЭЫЉрЭЫЉ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ | 2 . (8
div.p: As
div.formula: + рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ (рЭРє рЭЫЉрЭЫЉ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ ) вА† ) = вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ вЖУ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭРє вЖСвЖУ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ + рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР рЭСЧ вЖУ (рЭРє вЖСвЖУ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ ) вА† ) (рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ + рЭСЙ рЭСЧрЭСЦ ).(9)
div.p: In the following, we will take the nearest neighbor interaction to be рЭСЙ рЭСЧрЭСЦ = рЭСЙ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ = рЭСЙ вЙ§ 0 (corresponding to an attractive interaction) in the superconductor and zero elsewhere. The mean-field extended tight binding Hamiltonian now takes the form
div.formula: рЭРї рЭСЖрЭРґ рЭСЪ рЭСУ = рЭРї 0 - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ, рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉ рЭС° рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ, рЭЫЉ рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ рЭСЫ рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ + вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ ќФ рЭСЦ + рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС ќФ * рЭСЦ )+ 2 вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭСЙ (рЭСР вА† рЭСЧ вЖУ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭРє вЖСвЖУ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ + рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР рЭСЧ вЖУ (рЭРє вЖСвЖУ рЭСЦ рЭСЧ ) вА† ),(10)
div.p: where рЭРї 0 = рЭРї рЭСЖ 0 + рЭРї рЭСС 0 . The metallic ferromagnets that are attached to the superconductor are described by the following tight binding Hamiltonian
div.formula: рЭРї рЭРє рЭСА = - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ, рЭСЧ , рЭЫЉ рЭС° рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЧ рЭЫЉ - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ, рЭЫЉ рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ рЭСЫ рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ - вИСпЄБ рЭСЦ рЭЫњ рЭЫЉрЭЫљ вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ (рЭЬО рЭСІ ) рЭЫЉрЭЫљ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЦрЭЫљ .(11)
div.p: The last term represents the coupling between the spin of an electron at site рЭСЦ and the local magnetic exchange field, giving rise to ferromagnetism. The local exchange field вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ is taken to produce a spin-splitting in the рЭСІ-direction in spin-space, giving rise to a magnetization that could in general be either in-plane or out-of-plane in real space. Our model does not separate these cases as the magnetism is simply introduced through a spin-splitting. Orbital effects on the superconductor arising from the magnets, not considered in this model, can be limited by keeping the magnetization in-plane [33]. The Pauli matrices are denoted by рЭЬО, and the index рЭЫњ separates the local exchange field of each of the two magnets with рЭЫњ = рЭРњ, рЭСЕ for the leftmost and rightmost magnet, respectively. The sign of the local exchange field can be either the same or opposite for the two magnets, giving rise to parallel (P) or antiparallel (AP) ferromagnets. Outside of the magnets, the local magnetic exchange field is set to zero. The coupling between the magnets and the superconductor is introduced by having a nonzero hopping amplitude рЭС° рЭСЦ рЭСЧ across the ferromagnet-superconductor interfaces. The region outside of the superconductor and magnets is considered to be vacuum and decoupled from the rest of the system with a vanishing hopping amplitude.
After diagonalization, the free energy of the system will be expressed as
div.formula: рЭРє = рЭРї 0 - 1 2 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 рЭРЄ рЭСЫ - 1 рЭЫљ 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 ln(1 + рЭСТ -рЭЫљрЭРЄ рЭСЫ ),(12)
div.p: where рЭРЄ рЭСЫ is the quasiparticle energy associated with quantum number рЭСЫ, and рЭСБ is the number of lattice sites. The magnetic exchange interaction is computed as the difference in free energy between the configurations with parallel and antiparallel magnets
div.formula: рЭРљ = рЭРє вЖСвЖС -рЭРє вЖСвЖУ ,(13)
div.p: which includes both the RKKY interaction mediated by the quasiparticles as well as the effect of the magnetic configurations on the condensation energy of the superconductor.

The Hamiltonian рЭРї = рЭРї рЭСЖрЭРґ + рЭРї рЭРє рЭСА is diagonalized by means of the BdG method in order to compute the eigenvalues рЭРЄ рЭСЫ and eigenstates рЭЫЊ рЭСЫ . The diagonalized Hamiltonian will then take the form
div.formula: рЭРї = рЭРї 0 - 1 2 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 рЭРЄ рЭСЫ + 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 рЭРЄ рЭСЫ рЭЫЊ вА† рЭСЫ рЭЫЊ рЭСЫ .(14)
div.p: In order to perform the diagonalization, we start by rewriting the Hamiltonian as рЭРї = рЭРї 0 + 1 2 рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭРµ вА† рЭСЦ вДО рЭСЦ рЭСЧ рЭРµ рЭСЧ where we have introduced the basis
div.formula: рЭРµ вА† рЭСЦ = рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР вА† рЭСЦвЖУ рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС рЭСР рЭСЦвЖУ .(15)
div.p: Here, вДО рЭСЦ рЭСЧ is a 4 √Ч 4 matrix that takes the following form for
div.formula: рЭСЦ вЙ† рЭСЧ вДО рЭСЦ рЭСЧ = п£Ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£∞ -рЭС° 0 0 -2рЭСЙ рЭРє рЭСЦ рЭСЧ 0 -рЭС° 2рЭСЙ рЭРє рЭСЧрЭСЦ 0 0 2рЭСЙ (рЭРє рЭСЦ рЭСЧ ) * +рЭС° 0 -2рЭСЙ (рЭРє рЭСЧрЭСЦ ) * 0 0 +рЭС° п£є п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£ї ,(16)
div.p: and for рЭСЦ = рЭСЧ
div.formula: вДО рЭСЦ рЭСЧ = п£Ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£ѓ п£∞ -рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ + рЭЫњ вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ 0 0 ќФ рЭСЦ 0 -рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ -рЭЫњ вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ -ќФ рЭСЦ 0 0 -(ќФ рЭСЦ ) * +рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ -рЭЫњ вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ 0 (ќФ рЭСЦ ) * 0 0 +рЭЬЗ рЭСЦ + рЭЫњ вДО рЭЫњ рЭСЦ п£є п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£Ї п£ї . (17
div.p: Writing the Hamiltonian on matrix form рЭРї = рЭРї 0 + 1 2 рЭСК вА† рЭСЖрЭСК, and introducing the matrix P, we diagonalize the Hamiltonian
div.formula: рЭРї = рЭРї 0 + 1 2 рЭСК вА† рЭСГ вА† рЭСГрЭСЖрЭСГ вА† рЭСГрЭСК = рЭРї 0 + 1 2 W вА† рЭСЖ рЭСС W. The eigenvectors of рЭСЖ are ќ¶ вА† рЭСЫ = рЭЬС * 1рЭСЫ вАҐ вАҐ вАҐ рЭЬС * рЭСЦрЭСЫ вАҐ вАҐ вАҐ рЭЬС * рЭСБ рЭСЫ , рЭЬС * рЭСЦрЭСЫ = рЭЬР * рЭСЦрЭСЫ рЭЬИ * рЭСЦрЭСЫ рЭЬФ * рЭСЦрЭСЫ рЭЬТ * рЭСЦрЭСЫ ,(18)
div.p: such that
div.formula: рЭСГ вА† = ќ¶ 1 ќ¶ 2 . . . ќ¶ 4рЭСБ .(19)
div.p: We next use
div.formula: рЭСГ вА† W = рЭСК along with W вА† = рЭЫЊ вА† 1 . . . рЭЫЊ вА† 4рЭСБ ,(20)
div.p: and the relations between the quasiparticle operators that are not independent of each other.

As we are interested in the effect of the midgap states on the indirect interaction between two ferromagnetic leads connected to the superconductor, establishing the presence of midgap states is of importance. This can be achieved by calculating the single particle local density of states (LDOS) which should have a peak around zero energy in the presence of midgap states. The number of charges on lattice site рЭСЦ is given by рЭЬМ рЭСЦ = рЭЫЉ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭСР рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ , but this quantity can also be expressed as
div.formula: рЭЬМ рЭСЦ = +вИЮ вИЂ -вИЮ
div.p: рЭСБ рЭСЦ (рЭРЄ) рЭСУ (рЭРЄ)dрЭРЄ. Here рЭСБ рЭСЦ (рЭРЄ) is the local density of states at site рЭСЦ, and рЭСУ (рЭРЄ) is the Fermi-Dirac distribution with energy рЭРЄ measured relative to the chemical potential. At рЭСЗ = 0, we have рЭСУ (рЭРЄ) = 1 for рЭРЄ < 0 and рЭСУ (рЭРЄ) = 0 when рЭРЄ > 0. Comparing the two above expressions for the number of charges on lattice site рЭСЦ, the LDOS can then be expressed as
div.formula: рЭСБ рЭСЦ (рЭРЄ) = 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 |рЭЬФ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 + | рЭЬТ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 рЭЫњ рЭРЄ + рЭРЄ рЭСЫ + |рЭЬР рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 + |рЭЬИ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 рЭЫњ рЭРЄ -рЭРЄ рЭСЫ . (24)
div.p: Another quantity of interest is the magnetization on lattice site рЭСЦ, рЭСі рЭСЦ = рЭСЇ рЭСЦ . Here, the spin operator is defined as
div.formula: рЭСЇ рЭСЦ = рЭЫЉрЭЫљ рЭСР вА† рЭСЦ рЭЫЉ рЭЭИ рЭЫЉрЭЫљ рЭСР рЭСЦрЭЫљ .
div.p: The magnetization in the рЭСІ-direction can then be expressed as
div.formula: рЭСА рЭСІ рЭСЦ = 2рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭСЫ=1 |рЭЬР рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 + | рЭЬТ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 -|рЭЬФ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 -|рЭЬИ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 рЭСУ рЭРЄ рЭСЫ + |рЭЬФ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 -| рЭЬТ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ | 2 .(25)

div.head: B. Computational Methods
div.p: The computational part of this study consists of numerically diagonalizing the Hamiltonian and self-consistently solving the equations for either the on-site superconducting gap (Eq. ( 22)) or the nearest neighbor pairing amplitudes (Eq. ( 23)), depending on whether the superconductor is taken to be of the isotropic рЭС†-wave type or the рЭСС-wave type. Iterative solution of these equations require an initial value for the gap function/pairing amplitudes, and a convergence criterion in order to determine when a solution has been obtained. In this work, the convergence criterion was that the relative change in the gap/pairing amplitudes from one iteration to the next should be less than 1 √Ч 10 -4 for the рЭСС-wave and 1 √Ч 10 -3 for the рЭС†-wave state. The initial values for the рЭСС-wave state are listed in the Appendix and the initial value for the рЭС†-wave gap ќФ was set to 0.5рЭС°.

div.head: III. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
div.p: We first investigate the presence of midgap surface states, i.e. zero energy states existing on an edge of a superconductor. As displayed in Fig. 2, we calculate the LDOS for different points on an рЭС†-wave and a рЭСС-wave superconductor without magnetic contacts. One of the points is located at the diagonal edge, one of the points is in the bulk, and the third point is on the lower horizontal edge. Only on the diagonal edge of the рЭСС-wave superconductor, Fig. 2 (b1), there is a peak around zero energy signaling the presence of midgap states. In this figure, the chemical potential has been set to рЭЬЗ рЭСЖ = 0.7рЭС°, which gives rise to an asymmetric density of states around рЭРЄ = 0 for our tight binding model as the gap in the electron spectrum is opened away from the middle of the band.

As the presence of midgap states has been established, we move on to results for the indirect interaction between magnetic leads attached to a normal metal, an рЭС†-wave superconductor, and finally a рЭСС-wave superconductor.

div.head: A. Normal State
div.p: To put the results for the superconductors into context, we start with the case of magnetic leads connected by a normal metal (рЭСЙ = рЭСИ = 0). The indirect exchange interaction рЭРљ is presented in Fig. 3. For the horizontal edge, the result is the expected RKKY oscillations that are damped with increasing distance between the magnets. For the diagonal edge, the results are more peculiar, showing an enhanced interaction when the electrodes are close to the endpoints of the diagonal edge. Investigating the LDOS for рЭРЄ = 0 in Fig. 4, the reason becomes clear. Close to the edges of the system, the LDOS increases in magnitude and exhibits Friedel-like oscillations due to the abruptly vanishing charge density at the edge. The oscillatory and increased LDOS close to the edges correspondingly affects the RKKY interaction when the electrodes are close to the edge.

FIG. 4: Normal metal: Local density of states (LDOS) for рЭРЄ = 0 at the diagonal edge in the absence of magnetic contacts. The system size is рЭРњ рЭС• рЭСБ = 40 and рЭРњ рЭС¶ рЭСБ = 40, рЭСЙ = рЭСИ = 0, and the chemical potential in the normal metal is 0.9рЭС°.

div.head: B. s-wave pairing
div.p: We then move on to the case of magnetic leads connected by an isotropic рЭС†-wave superconductor (рЭСЙ = 0). The results for the indirect exchange interaction are presented in Fig. 5. In this case, there are two competing effects: the conventional RKKY interaction and the blocking of the states that can mediate the interaction due to the gap around the Fermi level in the band structure. For a weak attractive interaction рЭСИ in the superconductor, the RKKY interaction dominates, giving rise to an oscillating behavior. For larger рЭСИ, the gap becomes larger and can block more of the states that can mediate the interaction between the magnets. The interaction then displays a damping behavior instead of oscillations, and an antiparallel configuration of the magnets is preferred [34]. For the diagonal edge, the electrodes have been kept further away from the endpoints of the edge. For a weak attractive interaction рЭСИ, the enhanced RKKY oscillations occurring when the electrodes are close to the end-points can, however, still be observed, as explained previously. On the other hand, when the strength of the attractive interaction is increased, increasing the superconducting gap, we see that рЭРљ is damped to zero for sufficiently large magnet separation also for the diagonal edge. Thus, in the s-wave case, the qualitative behavior of рЭРљ is the For the diagonal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed 13 lattice points away from the endpoint of the edge, while for the horizontal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed 2 lattice points away from the endpoint. same regardless of which edge we consider.

div.head: C. d-wave pairing
div.p: Finally, we consider the main result of this work, which is how the magnetic leads interact when separated by a рЭСС-wave superconductor (рЭСИ = 0). The results for the indirect interaction between the magnetic leads is presented in Fig. 6. For the horizontal edge, the interaction displays an oscillating behavior and varies in sign as a function of the distance between the magnetic contacts. The results for the diagonal edge, on the other hand, show a qualitatively different behavior. The system now always prefers alignment of the ferromagnets and the interaction varies little with distance. Further, increasing вДО рЭСЦ now leads to a larger difference in free energy between the parallel and antiparallel magnet configurations.

The result that a parallel magnet configuration is strongly favored for the diagonal edge is surprising as one would expect that the parallel configuration induces a larger magnetization in the superconductor, which suppresses the gap and lowers FIG. 6: d-wave : Indirect exchange interaction between magnetic leads connected to a diagonal (a) and a horizontal (b) edge of a рЭСС-wave superconductor. Here рЭЬЗ рЭСЖ = 0.7рЭС°, рЭЬЗ рЭРє = 1рЭС°, рЭСЙ/рЭС° = -1, рЭРњ рЭС•рЭРє = 2, рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭРє = 10 and рЭСИ = 0. For the diagonal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed only 2 lattice points away from the endpoint of the edge in order to maximize the number of data points. The рЭСС-wave diagonal edge results are not sensitive to how close the magnets are to the endpoints of the edge. Further рЭРњ рЭС•рЭСЖ = 34, рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭСЖ = 30. For the horizontal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed 10 lattice points away from the endpoint, and рЭРњ рЭС•рЭСЖ = 40, рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭСЖ = 20. the condensation energy. A particularly large induced magnetization in the superconductor should be expected in the presence of midgap states which can give rise to a giant magnetic moment when subjected to a spin-splitting [4,35,36]. In accordance with this, we find a sizeable induced magnetization on the diagonal edge. As previously discussed in the literature, the magnetization induced in a superconductor due to proximity to a ferromagnet can be either aligned or anti-aligned with the magnetization of the ferromagnet [37][38][39]. A physical picture for the origin of an anti-aligned induced magnetization is that there are contributions from Cooper pairs where one of the two electrons is located in the ferromagnet, aligned with the local magnetization, leaving a Cooper pair partner with opposite spin in the superconductor. In the present system the induced magnetization tends to be anti-aligned with the magnetization of the magnetic contacts, as displayed in Fig. 7 for the 11th lattice site (from the left) of the diagonal edge is presented in (d), showing that the midgap states are more suppressed for the parallel magnet configuration. We have here taken the same parameters as in Fig. 6 (a), apart from a larger exchange field of вДО рЭСЦ = 2рЭС° in order to more clearly show the differences between the two configurations.

The effect of the introduction of the magnets is, however, not solely to reduce the gap due to an induced effective spinsplitting in the superconductor. The induced spin-splitting also splits the midgap states away from their resonance point at zero energy, suppressing the midgap states. As the gap close to the edge to begin with is strongly suppressed by the midgap states, the effect of reducing the midgap states, causing the superconducting order parameter to recover at the edge, is stronger than the effect of the spin-splitting on the condensation energy. As the parallel configuration most effectively produces a spinsplitting in the superconductor, this configuration features the largest condensation energy, giving rise to the behavior that is observed in Fig. 6 (a).

Investigating the constant term in the Hamiltonian рЭРї 0 = рЭСЦ рЭРї 0,рЭСЦ , the difference between рЭРї 0,рЭСЦ for the parallel and antiparallel configurations is presented in Fig. 7 (c). The figure shows that рЭРї 0 , which is a positive quantity, is largest for the parallel configuration, corresponding to a larger gap. In turn, this produces a larger condensation energy that lowers the free energy of the system. From the figure, it is clear that the main contribution to the difference in condensation energy between the magnetic configurations comes from the transition region where the antiparallel configuration has a reduced edge magnetization. LDOS results from this region are presented in Fig. 7 (d). While the AP configuration in this region has a clear midgap peak around zero energy, the midgap states for the P configuration have been split and suppressed by the induced spin-splitting.

We close by discussing briefly experimental considerations and possible choices of materials for observation of the рЭСС-wave results presented in this paper. While the system sizes in the presented calculations are limited by computational considerations, the presented results are expected to be robust also for larger systems. As RKKY interaction typically decays below experimentally accessible values over short length-scales of the order of nanometers, the separation between the magnetic contacts typically needs to be kept small. This might however only apply to the RKKY dominated indirect interaction that we observe for the horizontal edge of the рЭСС-wave superconductor. The preference of alignment of the ferromagnets when attached to a diagonal edge of a рЭСС-wave superconductor is expected to also be observable for larger magnet separation as the indirect interaction in this case is not dominated by itinerant carriers, but rather arises from the parallel configuration more efficiently inducing a spin splitting, suppressing the localized midgap states. The distance the magnets can interact over is then limited by the length scale determining how far away from a magnet the midgap states still experience a spin-splitting. If the magnet separation is much larger than this decay length of the induced spin-splitting along the edge, the spin-splitting arising from each magnet decays before interacting with the spin-splitting arising from the other magnet. There is then no difference between the two magnet configurations when it comes to suppression of midgap states, and the parallel configuration is no longer favored. For an рЭС†-wave superconductor in proximity to a ferromagnet, the proximity-induced magnetization decays over a length scale of the superconducting coherence length [40]. A natural length scale for the decay of the induced spin-splitting in the present case would then be the effective coherence length corresponding to the strongly suppressed order parameter at the edge. As the coherence length is inversely proportional to the order parameter, the magnets will then be able to interact over distances considerably larger than the bulk coherence length.

Experimental investigation of our main finding would con-sist of attaching magnetic leads to a {110} edge of a рЭСС-wave superconductor. The indirect interaction between the magnets can then be established by determining the energy barrier of switching between the two magnet configurations through an external magnetic field. Our prediction is that ferromagnetic alignment of the magnets will be preferred for a wide range of magnet separation distances. Possible material choices could be YBCO [29,41,42] for the рЭСС-wave superconductor featuring midgap states, and a nickel alloy like Ni 80 Co 20 [43] for the magnetic contacts.

div.head: IV. SUMMARY
div.p: We have investigated the indirect exchange interaction between two ferromagnetic leads connected to a superconductor as a function of the separation between the magnets, showing that the presence of zero energy surface states in a рЭСС-wave superconductor can qualitatively change the results. When the magnets are connected to an edge without zero energy surface states we find a normal oscillating RKKY behavior. However, when the magnets are connected to an edge featuring zero energy surface states, the strength of the magnetic exchange interaction is shifted away from zero, always favoring alignment of the magnetization in the two magnets, as the aligned configuration produces a larger superconducting condensation energy.

div.p: Using the unitary matrix рЭСГ рЭТМ the diagonalized form of the Hamiltonian will be рЭРї рЭСЖрЭРґ = рЭРї 0 + For different values of chemical potential and temperature, we then solve the self-consistent equations for the pairing amplitudes through iteration, using the different sets of initial values listed in table I. We then compare the resulting free energies (Eq. (A8)) and determine the favored phase of the system. The phase diagram is presented in Fig. 8. The choices for the initial values are determined by the expressions for the gap functions [44] ќФ рЭСС = (рЭСЙ/4) (рЭРє x+ + рЭРє x--рЭРє ≈Ј+ -рЭРє ≈Ј-),

ќФ

figure.head: FIG. 2 :FIG. 3 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2: Local density of states (LDOS) for different points of an рЭС†-wave superconductor (a) and a рЭСС-wave superconductor (b), showing the presence of midgap states on the diagonal edge of the рЭСС-wave superconductor. In both cases the size of the structure is рЭРњ рЭС•рЭСЖ = 34 and рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭСЖ = 30. For the рЭС†-wave results we have taken рЭСИ/рЭС° = 2 and рЭСЙ = 0, while for the рЭСС-wave results we have taken рЭСИ = 0 and рЭСЙ/рЭС° = -1. In both cases we have set the chemical potential рЭЬЗ рЭСЖ = 0.7рЭС°.

figure.head: FIG. 5
figure.figDesc: FIG. 5: s-wave : Indirect exchange interaction between magnetic leads connected to a diagonal (a) and a horizontal (b) edge of a рЭС†-wave superconductor. Here рЭЬЗ рЭСЖ = 0.9рЭС°, рЭЬЗ рЭРє = 1.2рЭС°, вДО рЭСЦ = 2рЭС°, рЭРњ рЭС•рЭСЖ = 40, рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭСЖ = 40, рЭРњ рЭС•рЭРє = 2, рЭРњ рЭС¶рЭРє = 10 and рЭСЙ = 0.For the diagonal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed 13 lattice points away from the endpoint of the edge, while for the horizontal edge, the leftmost magnet is fixed 2 lattice points away from the endpoint.

FIG.7: d-wave: Magnetization on each lattice site for the parallel (a) and antiparallel (b) configuration of magnets. The difference in рЭРї 0,рЭСЦ between the parallel and antiparallel configurations is presented in (c). The local density of states (LDOS) for the 11th lattice site (from the left) of the diagonal edge is presented in (d), showing that the midgap states are more suppressed for the parallel magnet configuration. We have here taken the same parameters as in Fig.6(a), apart from a larger exchange field of вДО рЭСЦ = 2рЭС° in order to more clearly show the differences between the two configurations.

figure.figDesc: x+ рЭРє x-рЭРє ≈Ј+ рЭРє ≈Ј- are the eigenvectors of рЭРї рЭТМ . The pairing amplitudes can then be expressed asрЭРє рЭС•¬± = 1 рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭСТ вИУрЭСЦрЭТМ вАҐ x рЭЬР рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭЬТ * рЭТМ, рЭЬО 1 -рЭСУ (рЭРЄ рЭТМ, рЭЬО ) + рЭСТ ¬±рЭСЦрЭТМ вАҐ x рЭЬФ * рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭЬИ рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭСУ (рЭРЄ рЭТМ, рЭЬО ) , рЭРє рЭС¶¬± = 1 рЭСБ вИСпЄБ рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭСТ вИУрЭСЦрЭТМ вАҐ ≈Ј рЭЬР рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭЬТ * рЭТМ, рЭЬО 1 -рЭСУ (рЭРЄ рЭТМ, рЭЬО ) + рЭСТ ¬±рЭСЦрЭТМ вАҐ ≈Ј рЭЬФ * рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭЬИ рЭТМ, рЭЬО рЭСУ (рЭРЄ рЭТМ, рЭЬО ) . figure.figDesc: FIG.8: Phase diagram for the tight-binding Hamiltonian with attractive nearest neighbor interaction between opposite spins рЭСЙ = -1рЭС°. Here, рЭСЗ is the temperature, рЭСШ рЭРµ is the Boltzmann constant, рЭЬЗ is the chemical potential, and рЭС° is the hopping amplitude.

The creation and annihilation operators {рЭСР вА† , рЭСР} can then be expressed in terms of quasiparticle creation and annihilation operators {рЭЫЊ вА† , рЭЫЊ} рЭСР рЭСЦвЖС = рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ рЭЬР рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ -рЭЬИ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ рЭЬФ * рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ ) рЭСУ (рЭРЄ рЭСЫ ) + рЭЬИ рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ рЭЬФ * рЭСЦ,рЭСЫ . The relationship between the normal electron operators and the quasiparticle operators is then рЭЬР рЭТМ,вЖС рЭЬР рЭТМ,вЖУ рЭЬФ * -рЭТМ,вЖС рЭЬФ

div.head: V. ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS
div.p: This work was supported by the Research Council of Norway through its Centres of Excellence funding scheme grant 262633 QuSpin.

div.head: Appendix A: Phase diagram
div.p: In order to choose the parameters such that the superconductor used in the study is in a рЭСС-wave state, we obtain a starting point by considering a square system with continuous boundary conditions in both the рЭС• and рЭС¶ directions and no attached magnetic leads. The relevant Hamiltonian is the one in Eq. ( 1) with рЭСИ рЭТК = рЭСЙ рЭТК рЭТЛ = 0. We introduce Fourier transformations for the electron operators рЭСР рЭТК рЭЫЉ = 1 вИЪ рЭСБ рЭТМ рЭСТ рЭСЦрЭТМ вАҐрЭТК рЭСР рЭТМ, рЭЫЉ where рЭТК = (рЭСЦ рЭС• , рЭСЦ рЭС¶ ) and рЭСБ is the number of lattice sites. After the mean-field approximation, Eq. ( 1) then becomes
Here
and we have defined,
